CSCE 481 Syllabus

Section 599 (42607)

Seminar

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: SEM

Meeting Days: TR

Start Time: 09:35AM

End Time: 10:50AM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  ONLINE ONLINE

Credit Hours:  1

Instructor Details

Pauline Wade

Email:  paulinewade@tamu.edu

Office Location:  PETR 108

Phone:  979 458 0480

Office Hours

Thursday 10-11 am

Preferred Contact Method

Email

Catalog Description

Seminar. (1-0). Credit 1. Investigation and report by students on topics of current

interest in computer science. Prerequisite: Junior or senior classification.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Junior or senior classification.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Understand career opportunities and pathways, such as graduate school, for new

graduates in computer science and computer engineering.

Apply techniques discussed when searching for jobs, interviewing, and negotiating.

Analyze ethical implications of professional decisions.

Special Course Designation

None

Grading Policy

The student’s semester grade will be based on criteria explained below.

The final grade will be based on the number of assigned activities you complete

successfully (as per given rubric or expectations), which can include (but not limited to)

synchronous or asynchronous, recorded seminars; ethics assignments and

assessments; other assigned activities at the instructor’s discretion.

The grade is calculated as number of activities successfully completed, divided by the

total number of activities. Except for excused absences, these points cannot be made

up, and extra credit will not be offered. The grading scale is shown below.

Letter Grade to Point Scale Mapping

For example, if there are 14 activities, you can miss one and still get an A in the

class, two absences a B, and three absences and a C.

For example, if there are 14 activities, you can miss one and still get an A in the

class, two absences a B, and three absences and a C.

Only Canvas submissions are accepted for credit. It is your responsibility to make

sure that the submission process is completed and that the correct assignment is

submitted to the correct place. It is best to double check your submission by

downloading it yourself, then confirming that the one stored in Canvas is the

intended one. Failure to confirm correct submission is not a valid excuse for

incomplete / late assignments

Guidelines for getting credit for synchronous seminars are described below, with

complete instructions provided in the assignment. Non-compliance with the

instructions will forfeit your credit for the seminar.

1.  Log into Zoom using SSO with your TAMU email, to allow us to match your login

details with the official Howdy roster. Instructions for Zoom SSO login will be

provided in Canvas.

2.  Make sure your zoom alias is: Last Name + COMMA + First Name, that match with

Howdy.

3.  Always keep your video on / face visible. We will be taking random screenshots

periodically during the talk.

4.  Answer polls in a timely manner, with each poll available for at least 1 minute. We

will have a total of 5 polls and missing more than 2 (i.e., 3 or more) will forfeit your

credit for the seminar.

5.  We have two separate channels of communication: Zoom for the actual seminar

and questions to the speaker, and Discord for student interaction. The links can be

accessed through Canvas. The reason for the separate channels is that questions

to the speaker often get buried in the Zoom chat.

6.  You need to be online for at least 60 (cumulative) minutes according to the Zoom

logs.

Please arrive early or on time and refrain from leaving early (you may miss

important announcements given towards the end of the class). If you experience

technical difficulties, you can just re-enter the session at your earliest opportunity.

It is your responsibility to make sure that you are in a place with good internet

connection and you have an operational device, and a backup device when

needed.

7.  You will submit a reflection with a minimum of 10 out of 18 points (~60%) to get

credit (see sample rubric in the next item). Please refer to the corresponding

reflection assignment and rubric for the specific scoring criteria which may be

connection and you have an operational device, and a backup device when

needed.

7.  You will submit a reflection with a minimum of 10 out of 18 points (~60%) to get

credit (see sample rubric in the next item). Please refer to the corresponding

reflection assignment and rubric for the specific scoring criteria which may be

different from what is shown below.

8.  Late reflections, submitted on 11:59 p.m. (or after), the day after the seminar, will

not be accepted for credit except for approved late submissions related to a

Student Rule 7 absence. You can request approval for late submissions using the

provided link in Canvas.

Please use the same link to request for an excused absence at least 3 business

days before your anticipated absence (except for emergencies). We will let you

know if the request is approved or not.

With regards to the reflection, a template & rubric will be provided in each

assignment. A sample reflection rubric with total points of 18 is shown below.

The word count is 150–220 words, with a 30-word buffer; 250 words is a  hard  limit,

and  no exceptions  will be made, even at 251 words.

Concise writing is a key professional skill because technical audiences need clear,

focused communication that respects their limited time. It trains you to prioritize

essential insights, state your main point clearly, and minimize filler, mirroring

expectations for workplace emails and reports.

The strict word limit also supports the course learning outcomes by requiring

specific, meaningful reflections that demonstrate your ability to analyze each

seminar, identify what matters most, and connect it to your own career and ethical

decisions.

Always verify your word count in Microsoft Word or in the Turnitin report (visible

after you submit). Word counts from Canvas, Microsoft Word, and Turnitin are the

only values that will be accepted.

Any evidence that the student did not solely write the reflection (Turnitin score greater

than 30%) is considered academic misconduct and will be reported to the honors

council and result in the application of an appropriate sanction. In addition,

after you submit). Word counts from Canvas, Microsoft Word, and Turnitin are the

only values that will be accepted.

Any evidence that the student did not solely write the reflection (Turnitin score greater

than 30%) is considered academic misconduct and will be reported to the honors

council and result in the application of an appropriate sanction. In addition,

submission of a reflection when not in attendance (in the case of a synchronous

seminar) can constitute academic misconduct.

You forfeit credit for the seminar when you do not meet the criteria previously

outlined, especially in the following situations:

You join the zoom meeting as a ‘guest.’

You don’t attend at all

Absences may only be excused as defined by the  Texas A&M University Student

Rules  available at  http://student-rules.tamu.edu/rule07 .  A student needs to

submit documentation for the absence (e.g., doctor’s note), using a link to be

provided by the instructor, no later than 1 week after a student return to

class, or the student will be automatically counted absent.

Offline for more than 15 minutes.

If  you have technical difficulties which leads to a loss of connection from the

virtual meeting , just simply rejoin. However, if you foresee that this situation

will occur frequently, you should seek a different location for future lectures. If

you are online for at least 60 minutes, you will be counted present.

Face not visible / video off

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Late work is not accepted.  Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused

absence (see Student Rule 7) is not considered late work and is exempted from the late

work policy. For excused absences, if the recording of the company talk is not available,

then the student will be given another makeup assignment by the instructor, which will

either be offline reading or watching another video.

Reasons for late submission requests not tied to absences (e.g., offline video submission)

must clearly be related to the criteria of excused absences according to  Student Rule 7 . The

instructor is under no obligation to accept late work if  not  related to an absence that is

excused under Student Rule 7.

No late work will be accepted after the last day of class, irrespective of the deadline for an

Reasons for late submission requests not tied to absences (e.g., offline video submission)

must clearly be related to the criteria of excused absences according to  Student Rule 7 . The

instructor is under no obligation to accept late work if  not  related to an absence that is

excused under Student Rule 7.

No late work will be accepted after the last day of class, irrespective of the deadline for an

assignment.

Course Schedule

Class will be scheduled every Tuesday (no Thursday class), unless notified otherwise, with the

final schedule posted on Canvas.

Additional Course Information

Course Expectations

Course expectations, in the form of a course appreciative agreement, will be reviewed and

agreed to at the beginning of the semester.

AI Statement

Writing: Use of AI tools, including ChatGPT, is permitted in this course for students who wish to

use them. To adhere to our scholarly values, students must cite any AI-generated material that

informed their work (this includes in-text citations and/or use of quotations, and in your

reference list). Using an AI tool to generate content without proper attribution qualifies as

academic dishonesty.

See  https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.

html  for information how to properly cite AI tools. (adapted from University of Massachusetts

Amherst,  https://cte.tamu.edu/getmedia/1d5e4ef6-97f1-4065-987f-3c9dfecbb7bd/TAMU-

CTE_GenAI-SyllabusStatementConsiderations.pdf )

Computer Code. In principle you may submit AI-generated code, or code that is based on or

derived from AI-generated code, as long as this use is properly documented in the comments:

you need to include the prompt and the significant parts of the response. AI tools may help

you avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that the generated code is correct. It is your

responsibility to identify errors in program logic through comprehensive, documented testing.

Moreover, generated code, even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for

improvement, in particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The

submission itself must meet our standards of attribution and validation.(from Boris Steipe

(2023) “Syllabus Resources”. The Sentient Syllabus Project  http://sentientsyllabus.org  .)
